questions.questionText: The normal tidal volume is approximately:
questions.explanation: Tidal volume (TV) ≈ 500 mL. Inspiratory reserve volume (IRV) ≈ 3000 mL. Expiratory reserve volume (ERV) ≈ 1100 mL. Residual volume (RV) ≈ 1200 mL. Vital capacity = TV + IRV + ERV ≈ 4600 mL. Total lung capacity = VC + RV ≈ 5800 mL. FRC = ERV + RV ≈ 2300 mL. Dead space ≈ 150 mL. Reference: Guyton — Lung Volumes.

questions.questionText: Functional residual capacity (FRC) is the volume of air remaining in the lungs at the end of:
questions.explanation: FRC = ERV + RV. Volume remaining after normal tidal expiration (~2300 mL). At FRC, elastic recoil of lungs (inward) = elastic recoil of chest wall (outward). FRC cannot be measured by simple spirometry (contains RV). Measured by helium dilution, nitrogen washout, or body plethysmography. FRC is increased in emphysema/COPD (air trapping). Decreased in restrictive diseases and obesity. Reference: Guyton — FRC.

questions.questionText: The volume that CANNOT be measured by simple spirometry is:
questions.explanation: Residual volume (and any capacity containing RV — FRC, TLC) cannot be measured by spirometry because air remaining after maximal expiration cannot be expelled. Measured by: helium dilution (closed circuit), nitrogen washout (open circuit), or body plethysmography (Boyle's law, most accurate). All other volumes and VC, IC, ERV can be measured by spirometry. Reference: Guyton — Spirometry.

questions.questionText: The normal alveolar-arterial (A-a) oxygen gradient is:
questions.explanation: Normal A-a gradient ≈ 5-15 mmHg (increases with age: normal = age/4 + 4). A-a gradient = PAO2 - PaO2. PAO2 (alveolar) calculated by alveolar gas equation: PAO2 = FiO2(Patm - PH2O) - PaCO2/R. Increased A-a gradient indicates: V/Q mismatch, shunt, or diffusion impairment. Normal A-a gradient with hypoxemia: hypoventilation or high altitude. Reference: Guyton — Gas Exchange.

questions.questionText: Surfactant is produced by:
questions.explanation: Type II pneumocytes (granular pneumocytes) produce and secrete surfactant (mainly DPPC — dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine). Surfactant reduces surface tension → prevents alveolar collapse at end-expiration, increases compliance, reduces work of breathing. Surfactant deficiency in premature infants → NRDS (neonatal respiratory distress syndrome). Mature surfactant production by 35 weeks (L:S ratio ≥2). Reference: Guyton — Surfactant.

questions.questionText: Hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction (HPV) is unique because in the lungs, hypoxia causes:
questions.explanation: HPV: hypoxia causes pulmonary arteriolar vasoconstriction (OPPOSITE to systemic circulation where hypoxia causes vasodilation). This diverts blood AWAY from poorly ventilated alveoli → optimizes V/Q matching. Mechanism: inhibition of K+ channels in pulmonary vascular smooth muscle → depolarization → Ca2+ entry → constriction. Pathological: generalized hypoxia (high altitude, COPD) → pulmonary hypertension → cor pulmonale. Reference: Guyton — HPV.

questions.questionText: The Hering-Breuer inflation reflex:
questions.optionA: Stimulates further inspiration when lungs are inflated
questions.optionB: Inhibits further inspiration when the lungs are adequately inflated
questions.explanation: Hering-Breuer inflation reflex: stretch receptors in bronchial smooth muscle → activated by lung inflation → vagal afferents → inhibit inspiratory neurons (DRG in medulla) → terminate inspiration → prevent overinflation. More important in neonates than adults. Deflation reflex: collapsed lungs stimulate inspiration. These are mechanoreflex/vagal reflexes, not chemoreceptor-mediated. Reference: Guyton — Respiratory Reflexes.

questions.questionText: The central chemoreceptors for respiratory control are located in the:
questions.explanation: Central chemoreceptors (ventrolateral medulla): respond primarily to H+ concentration in CSF (which reflects arterial PCO2 — CO2 crosses BBB → converted to H2CO3 → H+). Most important stimulus for normal breathing regulation. Peripheral chemoreceptors (carotid body — CN IX, aortic body — CN X): respond to decreased PaO2 (<60 mmHg), increased PaCO2, and decreased pH. Carotid body is more important for O2 sensing. Reference: Guyton — Respiratory Control.

questions.questionText: The most important stimulus for normal breathing in a healthy individual is:
questions.explanation: In normal individuals, PaCO2 is the most potent stimulus for ventilation. A rise of just 1 mmHg in PaCO2 increases ventilation by ~2-3 L/min. This acts mainly via central chemoreceptors. In COPD patients with chronic CO2 retention, central chemoreceptors become desensitized → 'hypoxic drive' takes over (peripheral chemoreceptors respond to low PaO2). Giving high-flow O2 to these patients can suppress their drive → CO2 narcosis. Reference: Guyton — Ventilatory Drive.

questions.questionText: The oxygen content of blood is carried mainly as:
questions.explanation: ~98.5% of O2 in blood is bound to Hb (oxyhemoglobin). Only ~1.5% is dissolved in plasma (PaO2 determines dissolved O2: 0.003 mL O2/dL per mmHg PaO2). Each gram of Hb can carry 1.34 mL O2 (Hüfner's constant). Total O2 content ≈ (1.34 × Hb × SaO2) + (0.003 × PaO2) ≈ 20 mL/dL. CO2 transport: 70% as HCO3-, 23% as carbaminohemoglobin, 7% dissolved. Reference: Guyton — O2 Transport.

questions.questionText: In a V/Q mismatch, a high V/Q ratio (like dead space) results in:
questions.explanation: V/Q = infinity → dead space effect (ventilation without perfusion — wasted ventilation). Example: pulmonary embolism. V/Q = 0 → shunt (perfusion without ventilation — blood not oxygenated). Example: atelectasis, pneumonia. Normal V/Q ≈ 0.8. V/Q is highest at the apex (high V, low Q) and lowest at the base (low V, high Q) of the upright lung. Reference: Guyton — V/Q Matching.

questions.questionText: The diffusing capacity of the lung (DLCO) is reduced in:
questions.explanation: DLCO reduced: emphysema (decreased surface area), pulmonary fibrosis (increased membrane thickness), anemia (less Hb), pulmonary embolism. DLCO increased: exercise (recruitment of capillaries), polycythemia (more Hb), pulmonary hemorrhage (blood in alveoli binds CO), asthma (mildly), left-to-right shunt (increased pulmonary blood flow). CO is used because it is entirely diffusion-limited (not perfusion-limited). Reference: Guyton — DLCO.

questions.questionText: Compliance of the lungs is:
questions.optionA: Change in pressure per unit change in volume
questions.optionB: Change in volume per unit change in pressure
questions.explanation: Compliance = ΔV/ΔP (mL/cmH2O). High compliance = lungs easily distensible (less effort needed). Normal lung compliance ≈ 200 mL/cmH2O. Increased compliance: emphysema (loss of elastic tissue). Decreased compliance: pulmonary fibrosis, ARDS, pulmonary edema, atelectasis. Elastance = 1/compliance (tendency to recoil). Surfactant increases compliance by reducing surface tension. Reference: Guyton — Lung Compliance.

questions.questionText: FEV1/FVC ratio is DECREASED in:
questions.explanation: Normal FEV1/FVC ≈ 80%. Obstructive: FEV1/FVC < 70% (FEV1 decreased proportionally more than FVC due to airflow limitation — asthma, COPD, bronchiectasis). Restrictive: FEV1/FVC normal or INCREASED (>80%, both FEV1 and FVC reduced proportionally — fibrosis, chest wall disease, neuromuscular). FEV1 alone is used to grade severity in COPD (GOLD criteria). Reference: Guyton — Spirometry Patterns.

questions.questionText: The anatomical dead space is approximately:
questions.explanation: Anatomical dead space ≈ 150 mL (~2.2 mL/kg or roughly 1 mL per pound of ideal body weight). It includes conducting airways (nose to terminal bronchioles) where no gas exchange occurs. Alveolar ventilation = (TV - dead space) × RR = (500-150) × 12 = 4200 mL/min. Physiological dead space = anatomical + alveolar dead space. Measured by Bohr equation: VD = VT × (PaCO2 - PECO2)/PaCO2. Reference: Guyton — Dead Space.

questions.questionText: Oxygen therapy can suppress respiratory drive in patients with:
questions.optionB: Chronic COPD with CO2 retention (hypoxic drive dependence)
questions.explanation: In chronic COPD with chronic hypercapnia, central chemoreceptors become desensitized to persistently high CO2. The patient relies on 'hypoxic drive' (peripheral chemoreceptors detecting low PaO2) for respiratory stimulation. High-flow O2 → corrects hypoxemia → removes hypoxic drive → hypoventilation → further CO2 retention → CO2 narcosis → coma. Use controlled low-flow O2 (24-28% via Venturi mask) in these patients. Reference: Guyton — Hypoxic Drive.

questions.questionText: The Bohr effect refers to:
questions.optionA: Increased O2 affinity of Hb at high pH
questions.optionB: Decreased O2 affinity of Hb at low pH (facilitating O2 unloading at tissues)
questions.optionC: Effect of CO2 on O2 binding in lungs
questions.explanation: Bohr effect: decreased pH (increased H+/CO2) → decreased Hb-O2 affinity → right shift of O2 dissociation curve → facilitates O2 unloading at tissues (where pH is lower, CO2 higher). Haldane effect: deoxygenated Hb binds more CO2 and H+ than oxygenated Hb → facilitates CO2 loading at tissues and CO2 unloading at lungs. Both effects enhance gas exchange efficiency. Reference: Guyton — Bohr Effect.

questions.questionText: In high altitude, the body compensates for low PO2 by:
questions.optionB: Hyperventilation, increased 2,3-DPG, polycythemia, increased capillary density
questions.explanation: High altitude acclimatization: (1) Acute: hyperventilation (carotid body stimulation by low PaO2) → respiratory alkalosis → renal compensation (HCO3- excretion). (2) Chronic: increased EPO → polycythemia, increased 2,3-DPG (right shift → better O2 unloading), increased capillary density, cellular adaptation (more mitochondria). Pulmonary hypertension (HPV) occurs. Acute mountain sickness/HAPE/HACE are maladaptive responses. Reference: Guyton — High Altitude.

questions.questionText: The pneumotaxic center is located in the:
questions.explanation: Pneumotaxic center (nucleus parabrachialis in upper pons): sends inhibitory signals to the DRG (dorsal respiratory group) in medulla → limits inspiration duration → regulates tidal volume and breathing rate. Strong pneumotaxic signals → rapid shallow breathing. Apneustic center (lower pons): has excitatory effect on DRG → promotes prolonged inspiration if unchecked by pneumotaxic center. DRG (inspiratory) and VRG (expiratory) are in the medulla. Reference: Guyton — Respiratory Centers.

questions.questionText: The work of breathing is increased in all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Normal quiet breathing requires minimal work (~3-5% of total body O2 consumption). Work increases with: decreased compliance (fibrosis, pulmonary edema, ARDS, obesity — elastic work increases), increased airway resistance (COPD, asthma — resistive work increases), increased ventilatory demand (exercise, fever). In severe respiratory disease, work of breathing can consume up to 30% of total O2 consumption. Reference: Guyton — Work of Breathing.

questions.questionText: Normal arterial PaO2 is:
questions.explanation: Normal ABG values: PaO2 = 80-100 mmHg, PaCO2 = 35-45 mmHg, pH = 7.35-7.45, HCO3- = 22-26 mEq/L, SaO2 > 95%. Hypoxemia: PaO2 < 80 mmHg. Type I respiratory failure: PaO2 < 60 mmHg with normal/low PaCO2. Type II (ventilatory failure): PaO2 < 60 + PaCO2 > 50 mmHg. PaO2 decreases with age (expected PaO2 ≈ 104 - age/3). Reference: Guyton — Normal ABG.

questions.questionText: Cyanosis becomes clinically apparent when the amount of deoxygenated hemoglobin exceeds:
questions.explanation: Central cyanosis becomes visible when deoxy-Hb > 5 g/dL in capillary blood. In severe anemia, cyanosis may NOT appear even with significant hypoxemia (insufficient total Hb to have >5 g deoxy-Hb). In polycythemia, cyanosis appears at higher SpO2 levels (more total Hb → easily reaches 5 g deoxy-Hb). Peripheral cyanosis: cold extremities, low flow states (deoxy-Hb increases locally). Reference: Guyton — Cyanosis.

questions.questionText: The closing volume increases with:
questions.explanation: Closing volume: lung volume at which small airways in dependent portions begin to close. Increases with age (in supine position, closing volume exceeds FRC by age ~44; upright by ~66 years), smoking, COPD (loss of radial traction on small airways). When closing volume exceeds FRC → air trapping, V/Q mismatch, hypoxemia during normal tidal breathing. This partly explains age-related decline in PaO2. Reference: Guyton — Closing Volume.

questions.questionText: The Haldane effect describes how:
questions.optionA: O2 binding decreases at low pH
questions.optionB: Deoxygenated Hb carries more CO2 than oxygenated Hb
questions.explanation: Haldane effect: deoxygenated Hb has a greater affinity for CO2 and H+ than oxygenated Hb. At tissues: Hb releases O2 → deoxyHb binds more CO2 (as carbaminoHb) and H+ → facilitates CO2 loading. At lungs: Hb binds O2 → releases CO2 and H+ → facilitates CO2 unloading. The Haldane effect accounts for ~50% of CO2 exchange. It is the 'mirror' of the Bohr effect. Reference: Guyton — Haldane Effect.

questions.questionText: Zone 1 of the West lung zones is characterized by:
questions.optionA: PA > Pa > Pv (alveolar pressure > arterial > venous — dead space)
questions.explanation: West lung zones (upright position): Zone 1 (apex): PA > Pa > Pv → no flow (dead space, normally minimal at rest). Zone 2 (middle): Pa > PA > Pv → intermittent flow (Starling resistor). Zone 3 (base): Pa > Pv > PA → continuous flow (most perfused). Zone 4: Pa > Pi > Pv > PA (interstitial pressure compresses vessels). Blood flow and ventilation both increase from apex to base, but perfusion increases faster. Reference: Guyton — West Zones.
